---
title: "Getting started"
subtitle: "What machine learning is — and is not"
---

## What is machine learning?

- Programs that improve from ==data== instead of hand-written rules
- Three main flavors:
  - **Supervised** learning — learn from labeled examples
  - **Unsupervised** learning — find structure without labels
  - **Reinforcement** learning — learn from rewards

::: {.callout-note title="Vocabulary"}
A **model** is a function with tunable parameters; **training** means finding
parameters that fit the data.
:::

## The workflow {.badge-important}

1. Collect and clean data
2. Split into **train** / **test** sets
3. Fit a model on the training set
4. Evaluate on the *held-out* test set

::: {.caption-note}
Never evaluate on the data you trained on — that only measures memorization.
:::

## Overfitting

- A model can be **too flexible**: it memorizes noise instead of learning signal
- Symptom: great on training data, ==poor on new data==
- Cure: more data, simpler models, regularization

## {background-color="white" .center .break-slide}

::: {.break-title}
Your first experiment
:::

## Hands-on: iris dataset {.badge-practice}

::: {.prompt}
Train a classifier on the iris dataset and report its test accuracy.
:::

```python
from sklearn.datasets import load_iris
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.neighbors import KNeighborsClassifier

X, y = load_iris(return_X_y=True)
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, random_state=0)

clf = KNeighborsClassifier(n_neighbors=3)
clf.fit(X_train, y_train)
print(clf.score(X_test, y_test))
```

## Where to go next {.badge-fyi}

- [Gradient descent](gradient-descent.qmd) — how models actually learn
- [Linear regression](linear-regression.qmd) — the simplest useful model
- scikit-learn user guide — excellent worked examples
